content.text: Visual Studio and Azure: The Azure Development Workload

Visual Studio (the full Windows IDE, not VS Code) ships with a dedicated Azure development workload that turns it into a complete Azure deployment and debugging environment. Installing the workload adds Azure project templates, the Azure Functions runtime, the Azurite storage emulator, Connected Services, and the Publish wizard — all integrated with Solution Explorer and the standard build pipeline.

This is distinct from VS Code with Azure extensions: Visual Studio targets .NET projects primarily, provides right-click publish flows, and integrates deeply with App Service, Azure Functions, and Service Fabric through first-party tooling maintained by Microsoft.

attrs.alt: Visual Studio Azure Tools integration overview

content.text: Installing the Azure Development Workload

The Azure development workload is selected during Visual Studio installation or added later through the Visual Studio Installer. It installs the Azure SDKs, project templates, emulators, and all Azure-aware tooling in one step.
Via Visual Studio Installer
Open Visual Studio Installer (search Start menu for 'Visual Studio Installer').
Click Modify next to your Visual Studio edition.
On the Workloads tab, check 'Azure development'.
Optionally check sub-components: Azure Functions tools, Service Fabric SDK, Azure WebJobs.
Click Modify and wait for the installer to complete.

# Silent install of VS 2022 with Azure workload via CLI
vs_installer_bootstrapper.exe install `
  --productId Microsoft.VisualStudio.Product.Community `
  --channelId VisualStudio.17.Release `
  --add Microsoft.VisualStudio.Workload.Azure `
  --add Microsoft.VisualStudio.Component.Azure.StorageEmulator `
  --quiet --norestart
Component
What It Installs
Required For
Azure development workload
Core Azure SDK, project templates, Cloud Explorer
Everything Azure in VS
Azure Functions tools
Functions runtime v4, local debugging, templates
Azure Functions projects
Service Fabric SDK
SF local cluster, SDK assemblies, templates
Service Fabric microservices
Azure WebJobs SDK
WebJob project type and SDK NuGet packages
Background job projects
Azurite (Storage Emulator)
Local Azure Storage emulator for blobs/queues/tables
Local storage dev without cloud
Connected Services

Connected Services is a Visual Studio feature (right-click project → Add → Connected Service) that wires your application to Azure backing services with minimal manual configuration. It installs the right NuGet packages, generates configuration code, creates or selects the Azure resource, and stores connection information in your project's secrets store.
Adding a Service Connection
Right-click the project in Solution Explorer → Add → Connected Service.
Choose a service category: Storage, Databases, Messaging, Security, AI/ML.
Select the specific service (e.g. Azure Blob Storage, Cosmos DB, Key Vault).
Sign in to Azure (or use an existing sign-in) and select/create the resource.
Choose where to store the connection string: local secrets, App Configuration, Key Vault.
Visual Studio installs the NuGet package and generates the wiring code.
Example: Adding Azure Blob Storage via Connected Services
Publishing to Azure with the Publish Wizard

The Publish wizard (right-click project → Publish) is the fastest way to deploy an ASP.NET Core or .NET application from Visual Studio to Azure App Service, Azure Container Apps, Azure Functions, or a VM. It creates a publish profile (.pubxml) that stores the deployment target configuration and can be reused for subsequent deployments.
Publish to App Service
Right-click the project → Publish.
Choose Azure as the target.
Choose Azure App Service (Windows) or (Linux).
Sign in and select/create the App Service instance.
Click Finish — Visual Studio creates the publish profile.
Click Publish to deploy. VS builds, packages, and pushes the app via WebDeploy or zip deploy.
The Publish Profile (.pubxml)

<!-- Properties/PublishProfiles/myapp-Azure.pubxml -->
<Project>
  <PropertyGroup>
    <PublishProtocol>Kudu</PublishProtocol>
    <PublishSiteName>myapp</PublishSiteName>
    <ResourceGroup>myRG</ResourceGroup>
    <ProjectGuid>...</ProjectGuid>
    <publishUrl>https://myapp.scm.azurewebsites.net:443</publishUrl>
    <msdeploySite>myapp</msdeploySite>
    <SkipExtraFilesOnServer>True</SkipExtraFilesOnServer>
    <MSDeployServiceURL>myapp.scm.azurewebsites.net:443</MSDeployServiceURL>
    <DeployIisAppPath>myapp</DeployIisAppPath>
    <RemoteSitePhysicalPath />
    <WebPublishMethod>MSDeploy</WebPublishMethod>
    <LastUsedBuildConfiguration>Release</LastUsedBuildConfiguration>
    <LastUsedPlatform>Any CPU</LastUsedPlatform>
    <SiteUrlToLaunchAfterPublish>https://myapp.azurewebsites.net</SiteUrlToLaunchAfterPublish>
    <LaunchSiteAfterPublish>True</LaunchSiteAfterPublish>
    <ExcludeApp_Data>False</ExcludeApp_Data>
    <TargetFramework>net8.0</TargetFramework>
    <RuntimeIdentifier>win-x64</RuntimeIdentifier>
    <SelfContained>false</SelfContained>
  </PropertyGroup>
</Project>
Automating Publish from the Command Line

# Publish using the saved profile (useful in CI)
dotnet publish MyApp.csproj `
  /p:PublishProfile=myapp-Azure `
  /p:Password=$env:DEPLOY_PASSWORD `
  -c Release
Azure Functions Development in Visual Studio

Visual Studio's Azure Functions tools provide a complete local development experience: the Functions runtime runs inside VS during F5 debugging, HTTP trigger functions get a local URL (http://localhost:7071), and timer/queue triggers can be invoked manually from the Functions panel.
Creating an Azure Functions Project
File → New → Project → search 'Azure Functions'.
Choose Azure Functions (C#) template.
Select the Functions runtime version (v4 for .NET 8 isolated worker).
Select the trigger type for the first function: HTTP, Timer, Service Bus Queue, Blob, etc.
Click Create.
local.settings.json — Local Configuration
local.settings.json is never deployed to Azure — it is for local development only. The equivalent values in Azure are set via Application Settings in the Function App configuration blade.
Deploying an Azure Functions Project

# Publish via Azure Functions Core Tools (called by VS internally)
func azure functionapp publish myFunctionApp --build remote

# Or publish directly from VS via right-click → Publish
# VS packages the isolated worker host + your functions DLL
# and deploys via zip deploy to the Function App SCM endpoint
Application Insights Integration

When an App Service or Functions project is published through Visual Studio, you can enable Application Insights directly from the publish dialog or from Solution Explorer → Connected Services → Azure Application Insights. This adds the SDK NuGet package and configures the instrumentation key/connection string automatically.
Live Metrics and Snapshot Debugger
With Application Insights connected, Visual Studio provides two powerful production debugging tools accessible from the Publish summary page:
Tool
How to Access in VS
What It Shows
Live Metrics Stream
Publish summary → Application Insights → Live Metrics
Real-time request rate, failure rate, server CPU/memory
Snapshot Debugger
Debug → Attach Snapshot Debugger
Captures local variables at the point of an exception in production
Log streaming
Publish summary → App Service Logs
Real-time stdout/stderr from the running app
Remote profiler
Debug → Profile Azure Web App
CPU flame graph from a live App Service instance
Cloud Explorer

Cloud Explorer (View → Cloud Explorer) is a Visual Studio tool window that lists your Azure subscriptions, resource groups, and resources in a tree similar to the Azure Portal. It allows common resource management operations directly from the IDE without switching to a browser.
View and open App Service web apps, Function Apps, storage accounts, and SQL databases.
Start, stop, and restart App Service instances.
View application logs and streaming logs for App Service.
Open resources directly in the Azure Portal.
Manage Azure Storage blobs, queues, and tables through a built-in storage browser.
Connect to Azure SQL databases using SQL Server Object Explorer.
Cloud Explorer is being deprecated in favor of VS Code with Azure extensions for some resource types. For most .NET App Service scenarios it remains fully functional in Visual Studio 2022.
Generating GitHub Actions CI/CD from Visual Studio

The Publish wizard can generate a GitHub Actions workflow file that replicates the publish profile's deployment steps in CI. This avoids writing the YAML from scratch and ensures the workflow uses the same deployment method (zip deploy, WebDeploy) that works locally.
Generate the Workflow
Publish the project at least once manually to create the publish profile.
Open the publish summary page (double-click the .pubxml in Solution Explorer).
Click 'Set up CI/CD with GitHub Actions'.
Sign in to GitHub and select the repository and branch.
Visual Studio commits the generated workflow YAML to .github/workflows/ in the repo.

# .github/workflows/azure-webapps-dotnet.yml — generated by VS
name: Deploy to Azure App Service

on:
  push:
    branches: [ "main" ]

jobs:
  build-and-deploy:
    runs-on: windows-latest

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Set up .NET
        uses: actions/setup-dotnet@v4
        with:
          dotnet-version: '8.0.x'

      - name: Build
        run: dotnet build --configuration Release

      - name: Publish
        run: dotnet publish -c Release -o ./publish

      - name: Deploy to Azure App Service
        uses: azure/webapps-deploy@v3
        with:
          app-name: 'myapp'
          publish-profile: ${{ secrets.AZURE_WEBAPP_PUBLISH_PROFILE }}
          package: ./publish

Bicep support
Via extension (VS Marketplace)
First-class Bicep extension

Windows, macOS, Linux

Community free; Pro/Enterprise paid
Free
Working with Azure Storage in Visual Studio

The Azure development workload installs Azurite — the cross-platform Azure Storage emulator — and provides a Storage browser UI through Cloud Explorer and the Azure Storage Explorer integration. You can browse, upload, download, and delete blobs, queue messages, and table entities without leaving Visual Studio.
Starting Azurite for Local Development

# Azurite is included with the Azure development workload
# Start from the command line (installed alongside VS)
azurite --silent --location C:\azurite --debug C:\azurite\debug.log

# Or start individual services
azurite-blob --silent --location C:\azurite\blob
azurite-queue --silent --location C:\azurite\queue
azurite-table --silent --location C:\azurite\table

# Connection string for local code pointing to Azurite
# (same as "UseDevelopmentStorage=true" shorthand)
"DefaultEndpointsProtocol=http;AccountName=devstoreaccount1;AccountKey=Eby8vdM02xNOcqFlqUwJPLlmEtlCDXJ1OUzFT50uSRZ6IFsuFq2UVErCz4I6tq/K1SZFPTOtr/KBHBeksoGMGw==;BlobEndpoint=http://127.0.0.1:10000/devstoreaccount1;QueueEndpoint=http://127.0.0.1:10001/devstoreaccount1;TableEndpoint=http://127.0.0.1:10002/devstoreaccount1"
Switching Between Emulator and Real Azure Storage
Managing App Service Deployment Slots from Visual Studio

Visual Studio's Publish wizard supports deployment slots — the staging/production swap workflow built into Azure App Service. You publish to a staging slot, verify the deployment, then swap to production from inside VS without opening the Portal.
Publishing to a Slot
Open the publish profile (.pubxml) or create a new one.
In the Publish wizard, expand 'Settings' and check 'Enable deployment slot'.
Enter the slot name (e.g. staging).
Publish — the deployment goes to myapp/staging, not to the production slot.

# Publish to a specific deployment slot via CLI
az webapp deploy   --resource-group myRG   --name myapp   --slot staging   --src-path ./publish.zip   --type zip

# Swap staging to production after validation
az webapp deployment slot swap   --resource-group myRG   --name myapp   --slot staging   --target-slot production
Slot-Specific Application Settings

# Mark a setting as slot-sticky (does not travel with swap)
az webapp config appsettings set   --resource-group myRG   --name myapp   --slot staging   --settings ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT=Staging   --slot-settings ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT  # mark as sticky

# Slot-sticky settings stay in the slot after a swap
# Use this for environment-specific connection strings and feature flags
Debugging Azure Functions Locally in Visual Studio

Pressing F5 with an Azure Functions project starts the Functions host process and attaches the VS debugger. All trigger types work locally — HTTP triggers are exposed on localhost:7071, timer triggers can be fired manually, and queue/blob triggers respond to messages sent to Azurite.
Setting Breakpoints and Inspecting Trigger Data
Triggering Non-HTTP Functions Manually

# Trigger a timer function manually via the Functions host admin API
# (only works when running locally with F5 in VS)
Invoke-RestMethod `
  -Uri "http://localhost:7071/admin/functions/DailyCleanup" `
  -Method POST `
  -Body '{}' `
  -ContentType "application/json"

# For queue-triggered functions — send a message to Azurite queue
$ctx = New-AzStorageContext -ConnectionString "UseDevelopmentStorage=true"
$queue = Get-AzStorageQueue -Name "orders" -Context $ctx
if (-not $queue) {
    New-AzStorageQueue -Name "orders" -Context $ctx
}
$queueRef = $queue.CloudQueue
$queueRef.AddMessage([Microsoft.Azure.Storage.Queue.CloudQueueMessage]::new('{"orderId":"test-001"}'))
Managing Azure SQL Databases from Visual Studio

Visual Studio's SQL Server Object Explorer (View → SQL Server Object Explorer) connects to Azure SQL databases alongside local SQL Server instances. You can browse schemas, run queries, compare schemas, and publish dacpac files directly from the IDE.
Connecting to Azure SQL
View → SQL Server Object Explorer.
Click the 'Add SQL Server' button (plug icon).
In the Connect dialog, enter your Azure SQL server hostname (myserver.database.windows.net).
Choose Azure Active Directory authentication or SQL authentication.
Expand the connection to browse databases, tables, and stored procedures.
Publishing Database Schema Changes via DACPAC

# Build a dacpac from a Visual Studio Database project
msbuild MyDatabase.sqlproj /t:Build /p:Configuration=Release
# Output: bin/Release/MyDatabase.dacpac

# Publish the dacpac to Azure SQL
SqlPackage.exe /Action:Publish `
  /SourceFile:bin/Release/MyDatabase.dacpac `
  /TargetServerName:myserver.database.windows.net `
  /TargetDatabaseName:mydb `
  /TargetUser:sqladmin `
  /TargetPassword:$env:SQL_PASSWORD `
  /p:BlockOnPossibleDataLoss=false

# Schema compare: Azure SQL vs local dacpac
# From SQL Server Object Explorer → right-click database → Schema Compare
# Or from command line:
SqlPackage.exe /Action:DeployReport `
  /SourceFile:bin/Release/MyDatabase.dacpac `
  /TargetServerName:myserver.database.windows.net `
  /TargetDatabaseName:mydb `
  /TargetUser:sqladmin `
  /TargetPassword:$env:SQL_PASSWORD `
  /OutputPath:DeployReport.xml
Common Pitfalls and Troubleshooting
Problem
Cause
Fix
Publish fails with 'ERROR_USER_UNAUTHORIZED'
Web Deploy credentials changed or expired
Re-enter publish credentials: re-download publish profile from App Service → Publishing credentials
Functions F5 fails — 'Unable to start host'
Port 7071 in use or wrong runtime version
Change LocalHttpPort in local.settings.json; verify Functions runtime version matches project target
Connected Services shows no Azure resources
Not signed in to the correct tenant
Tools → Options → Azure Service Authentication → select account with correct tenant
Azurite tables not connecting
Table service port 10002 blocked or not started
Start Azurite with azurite-table separately; check firewall allows 10002
Snapshot Debugger not attaching
App Service plan is Basic or lower
Snapshot Debugger requires Standard tier or above; scale up the App Service plan
Publish profile auth fails in GitHub Actions
Publish profile secret missing/expired
Re-download from Portal → Deployment Center → Manage publish profile, update GitHub secret
Summary

Visual Studio's Azure development workload delivers a tightly integrated .NET-to-Azure deployment experience: Connected Services wires backing services with a few clicks, the Publish wizard handles App Service and Functions deployments without leaving the IDE, and built-in Application Insights tooling enables production debugging through Live Metrics and Snapshot Debugger. For .NET-first teams working on Windows, it reduces the context-switching between IDE, portal, and CLI that characterizes other development workflows.
